stem: A 19-year-old client is 24 hours post-injury from a comminuted femur fracture and develops acute respiratory distress. Based on the client's electronic health record, complete the bowtie. Select the potential condition, two actions to take, and two parameters to monitor.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content: S: At 14:00, I'm calling about the 19-year-old male in traction for a femur fracture who has acute respiratory distress and confusion. B: He sustained a comminuted femur fracture 24 hours ago in a motor vehicle accident. A: He is agitated, with an SpO2 of 84% on 4L O2 via nasal cannula. Vitals are HR 122/min, RR 32/min, T 38.6°C. He has a new petechial rash on his chest. R: I recommend immediate provider evaluation for suspected Fat Embolism Syndrome.

tabs.title: Vital Signs
tabs.content:
Time: 14:00
BP: 108/62 mmHg
HR: 122/min
RR: 32/min
T: 38.6° C (101.4° F)
SpO2: 84% on 4 L/min via nasal cannula

tabs.title: Labs
tabs.content:
Previous Day - 16:00
Hemoglobin: 14.5 g/dL (13.2-17.3)
Platelets: 250,000/mm³ (150,000-400,000)

Today - 14:15
Arterial Blood Gas (ABG): Pending
Complete Blood Count (CBC): Pending

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content: Chest X-ray (14:30): Bilateral diffuse 'snowstorm' infiltrates noted.

tabs.title: MAR
tabs.content:
PRN Medications:
Morphine 2 mg IV every 4 hours PRN pain

Continuous Treatments:
Oxygen 4 L/min via nasal cannula, continuous

conditionOptions.hoverRationale: This is incorrect. While DVT is a risk, the client's acute respiratory failure, neurological changes, and petechial rash are not characteristic of DVT. These symptoms are the classic triad for Fat Embolism Syndrome.

This is correct. Fat globules released from the fractured long bone have traveled to the pulmonary and systemic circulation, causing the classic triad of respiratory distress, neurological decline, and a petechial rash.

This is incorrect. Compartment syndrome involves increased pressure within a muscle compartment, causing severe pain, pallor, and pulselessness in the affected limb. The client's symptoms are systemic, not localized to the limb.

actionOptions.text: Administer high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask.
actionOptions.hoverRationale: This is a priority action. The client's hypoxemia (SpO2 84%) is due to fat emboli obstructing pulmonary capillaries. High-flow oxygen is essential to correct hypoxemia and improve tissue oxygenation.

actionOptions.text: Ensure strict immobilization of the fractured extremity.
actionOptions.hoverRationale: This is a crucial action. Minimizing movement of the fractured femur prevents the release of additional fat globules from the bone marrow into the bloodstream, which would worsen the client's condition.

This is incorrect. Heparin is an anticoagulant used for thromboembolism (blood clots), not fat emboli. Administering heparin would not treat the underlying cause and would increase the risk of bleeding.

actionOptions.text: Instruct the client to perform leg exercises.
actionOptions.hoverRationale: This is contraindicated. Instructing the client to perform leg exercises would increase movement of the fractured bone, potentially dislodging more fat globules into the circulation and exacerbating the fat embolism.

parameterOptions.text: Respiratory rate, SpO2, and lung sounds.
parameterOptions.hoverRationale: This is a priority parameter. Monitoring respiratory status is critical to assess the severity of pulmonary dysfunction and the client's response to oxygen therapy, as FES can rapidly progress to ARDS.

parameterOptions.text: Neurological status and presence of petechiae.
parameterOptions.hoverRationale: This is a priority parameter. Monitoring for changes in consciousness and the progression of the petechial rash is essential, as these signs indicate the extent of systemic embolization to the brain and skin capillaries.
This is not a relevant parameter. While corticosteroids may be used in FES management, monitoring blood glucose is not a primary parameter for tracking the progression of the syndrome itself.
This is not a relevant parameter. Trousseau's sign is an indicator of hypocalcemia, which is not associated with the pathophysiology or clinical presentation of Fat Embolism Syndrome.
